input.command:
cd /Users/raman/projects/lock_code_manager
git add .github/workflows/release.yaml
git commit -q -F - <<'EOF'
fix(ci): derive release tag from the release payload, not github.ref

The Release workflow took the tag from `github.ref`, which is unreliable for
`release` events: it can resolve to the target branch instead of the tag. When
that happens (run 27973036902 for 4.1.4, triggered with ref=main), the upload
step's `tag: ${{ github.ref }}` came through empty and svenstaro/upload-release-action
failed with "Input required and not supplied: tag". The same run would also have
stamped a garbage version (`refs/heads/main`) into const.py/manifest.json via the
Get Version step's `${GITHUB_REF/refs\/tags\//}` substitution.

Use `github.event.release.tag_name` for both the version stamp and the upload tag;
it is always the published release's tag regardless of the triggering ref. The
Get Version step reads it via an env var (not inline in run:) per workflow-injection
hardening.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
git push -u origin fix/release-tag-from-event 2>&1 | tail -2
input.description: Commit and push the workflow fix

input.command:
cd /Users/raman/projects/lock_code_manager
cat > /tmp/pr_body_ci.md <<'EOF'
## Proposed change

The Release workflow derived the tag from `github.ref`, which is **unreliable for `release` events** — it can resolve to the target branch instead of the tag. When that happened (run [27973036902](https://github.com/raman325/lock_code_manager/actions/runs/27973036902) for 4.1.4, triggered with ref `main`), the upload step's `tag: ${{ github.ref }}` came through empty and `svenstaro/upload-release-action` failed with `Input required and not supplied: tag`.

The same condition is a latent second bug: the `Get Version` step's `${GITHUB_REF/refs\/tags\//}` substitution would have stamped `refs/heads/main` as the version into `const.py`/`manifest.json` — the upload failing is the only reason a garbage-versioned asset wasn't produced. Every successful release run was triggered with the ref pointing at the tag; only the run where it pointed at `main` failed.

This switches both the version stamp and the upload tag to `github.event.release.tag_name`, which is always the published release's tag regardless of the triggering ref. The `Get Version` step reads it via an `env:` var rather than interpolating into the `run:` script, per workflow-injection hardening.

To recover the missing 4.1.4 asset, re-run that release's job after this merges.

## Type of change

- [x] Bugfix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue)
- [ ] New feature (which adds functionality)
- [ ] Breaking change (fix/feature causing existing functionality to break)
- [ ] Code quality improvements to existing code or addition of tests

## Additional information

- Fixes the intermittent Release-workflow failure seen on the 4.1.4 release run.
EOF
gh pr create --title "fix(ci): derive release tag from the release payload, not github.ref" --body-file /tmp/pr_body_ci.md --base main 2>&1 | tail -3

aiTitle: Fix circuit breaker trips for matter locks

content.text:
**PR #1292** is open: https://github.com/raman325/lock_code_manager/pull/1292

## Summary

Fixed both bugs in `.github/workflows/release.yaml`:
- **Upload tag** (`tag: ${{ github.ref }}` → `${{ github.event.release.tag_name }}`) — the empty-`tag` cause of the 4.1.4 failure.
- **Version stamp** (`Get Version` now reads `github.event.release.tag_name` via an `env:` var, not `${GITHUB_REF/...}`) — closes the latent "garbage version written into `const.py`/`manifest.json`" path, and follows workflow-injection hardening (no untrusted value interpolated into `run:`).

Verified: yamllint + the GitHub Actions linter (`actionlint`) pass via `prek`.

**To recover the missing 4.1.4 asset:** once #1292 merges, re-run the failed job on run [27973036902](https://github.com/raman325/lock_code_manager/actions/runs/27973036902) — though note a re-run uses the workflow at the commit the release points to, so if that doesn't pick up the fix, the simplest path is to delete and re-publish the 4.1.4 release (the tag already exists), which will trigger the corrected workflow.

One caveat worth flagging: the underlying GitHub behavior — a `release` event arriving with the ref pointing at `main` instead of the tag — is intermittent and outside our control; this change just makes the workflow immune to it. So if you ever see a release run where checkout grabs `main`'s SHA, that's the same condition, now handled.
